files.SKILL.md:
# Anchor Questions

Good work is held in place by the questions you remember to ask.
What follows is a small set, grouped by where in the work they
belong. None are optional. Skipping one is how careful work turns
into a confident-sounding mistake.

## Before you begin

*What does the request literally say?*

Read the words on the page, not the request you expected to
receive. Notice closing instructions and quiet prohibitions —
"don't send," "don't change," "draft only." These bind every
action that follows.

*What surfaces might hold relevant data?*

List them before reaching for any one. People leave traces in
more than one place: messages, schedules, trackers, official
records, prior notes, reference material. A picture built from a
single source is half a picture.

*Are there notes from earlier work?*

If they exist, skim them. Treat them as suggestions of where to
look, not as facts about today. The world has moved since they
were written.

## While you gather

*Where did this value come from?*

For every name, identifier, time, amount, or status you intend to
use, point to the specific record that produced it. If you can
only say "I saw something like this earlier," the value is not
yet in your hand. Read it again.

*What do the sources say when compared?*

When two records agree, you have a fact. When they disagree, you
have a question worth surfacing. When one is silent, note the
absence — it is often the most important finding in the set.

*What is missing that should be there?*

A stakeholder who has not weighed in. A decision that was
deferred and never revisited. A status that was promised but
never updated. Gaps deserve as much attention as the visible
content.

## Before you sort

*What is actually urgent, and what merely feels urgent?*

A live problem affecting real people outweighs an administrative
request. A passed deadline outweighs an approaching one. An item
that blocks others outweighs one that blocks nothing.

*Who is the audience for each piece of this?*

Some facts belong to the people doing the work. Some belong to
the people overseeing it. Some belong to people outside the
organization. The same piece of information rarely belongs to all
three at the same level of detail.

*What in this material is sensitive?*

Internal system names, record identifiers, personnel matters,
financial exposures, anything labelled confidential or private.
These do not cross from internal contexts into external or
broadly shared ones.

## Before you act

*Has the owner of this work approved this action?*

If the request says draft, draft and stop. If it says do not
send, do not send. Read-only analysis is finished work when that
is what was asked. There are no test posts, no placeholder
messages, no throwaway artifacts — every write is real and
visible the moment it lands.

*Can each claim I am about to make be traced to something I
read?*

If yes, proceed. If no, either fetch what is missing or remove
the claim. State absences plainly rather than guessing values
that sound right.

*Is this commitment a real one?*

A real commitment has a concrete time or window and a named
owner. "Soon," "shortly," "in due course" are not commitments;
they are promises to disappoint someone later.

*Should this be one record or several?*

Separate items belong in separate records. One next action per
record, one owner per record. Bundles get skimmed and forgotten.

*Where does the official record need to land?*

If the topic has a tracker, calendar, or system of record,
updates belong there as well as in conversation. A message
without a corresponding entry is half-finished work.

*Who else has stake in this topic?*

When multiple groups care about the same thing, each needs to
hear it directly. A post in one place leaves the others blind.

## After you act

*Did the change actually land?*

A success response is not always proof. Look at the thing you
changed. One extra read catches an entire class of error that
nothing else will.

*If something failed, do I understand why?*

If a call failed on its structure, do not retry the same shape;
change the approach. If an action appears to succeed but leaves
no trace, stop and find out why before continuing.

## Before you finish

*What did this teach me that would help with similar future
work?*

If there is a pattern, a structural observation, or a decision
rule worth remembering, write it down compactly. Focus on shapes
and rules, not on the names, identifiers, or content of this
particular task — those rotate and stored copies of them will
mislead later.

*Does this update something I previously believed?*

If so, update or supersede the older entry. Prefer recent
observations. Keep the total volume of notes small enough to read
at the start of future work without effort.

*Is there nothing new to record?*

Then do not force a note. An empty reflection is better than
noise.

## Questions worth refusing

*Could I just handle this adjacent thing too?*

Probably yes, and probably you should not. The instruction is
narrower than what the data invites. Honor the narrower reading.
Note anything truly important that falls outside scope; do not
act on it unprompted.

*Should I summarise the plan instead of doing the work?*

No. A description of what you are about to do is not a substitute
for doing it.

*Can I rely on what I think I remember about this kind of
situation?*

No. Even familiar-looking work requires fresh evidence from fresh
reads. Memory of similar tasks is a starting hypothesis, not a
shortcut around the data.
